en.extract:
The Simeulue parrot (Psittinus abbotti) is a parrot endemic to two small islands off Sumatra. It was formerly considered a subspecies of the blue-rumped parrot (Psittinus cyanurus), but is split by the HBW/BirdLife checklist, with the IOC later following suit, supporting its status as a distinct species.


Description
The Simeulue parrot is a small, short-tailed bird of stocky build, measuring approximately 19 cm (7.5 in). It has a bright green colouration, which in adult males is accented by a blue face with a green crown and a black collar. It differs from the blue-rumped parrot in its larger size, the lack of grey mantle and back, lack of blue rump, green instead of brown head colouration in the female, and blue head colouration in the male.


Distribution and conservation status
This taxon is restricted to Simeulue and Siumat islands off north-west Sumatra. Estimates of total population size are uncertain, ranging from 5,000 up to 47,000 birds. While there is no current indication of population decline, the Simeulue parrot has been classified as near threatened by the IUCN due to its limited distribution and likely present and future loss of habitat to mining and agriculture. It is the only Indonesian parrot species not protected by Indonesian law.

The '''Simeulue parrot''' ('''''Psittinus abbotti''''') is a [[parrot]] endemic to two small islands off [[Sumatra]]. It was formerly considered a subspecies of the [[blue-rumped parrot]] (''Psittinus cyanurus''), but is split by the [[HBW and BirdLife International Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World|HBW/BirdLife checklist]], with the [[International Ornithologists' Union|IOC]] later following suit, supporting its status as a distinct species.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Species Updates – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/updates/species-updates/|access-date=2021-06-13|language=en-US}}</ref>

==Description==
The Simeulue parrot is a small, short-tailed bird of stocky build, measuring approximately {{cvt|19|cm}}. It has a bright green colouration, which in adult males is accented by a blue face with a green crown and a black collar. It differs from the blue-rumped parrot in its larger size, the lack of grey mantle and back, lack of blue rump, green instead of brown head colouration in the female, and blue head colouration in the male.<ref name="iucn" /><ref name=hbw>{{cite taxon|BOW |title=Simeulue Parrot (''Psittinus abbotti'') |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/burpar5/cur/introduction |date=2023 |last1=Collar |first1=Nigel |last2=Del Hoyo |first2=Josep |last3=Kirwan |first3=Guy M.}}</ref>

==Distribution and conservation status==
This taxon is restricted to [[Simeulue]] and [[Siumat]] islands off north-west [[Sumatra]]. Estimates of total population size are uncertain, ranging from 5,000 up to 47,000 birds. While there is no current indication of population decline, the Simeulue parrot has been classified as [[near-threatened species|near threatened]] by the [[IUCN]] due to its limited distribution and likely present and future loss of habitat to mining and agriculture.<ref name="iucn" /> It is the only Indonesian parrot species not protected by Indonesian law.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nandika |first1=Dudi |last2=Agustina |first2=Dwi |last3=Heinsohn |first3=Robert |last4=Olah |first4=George |date=2021-09-30 |title=Wildlife Trade Influencing Natural Parrot Populations on a Biodiverse Indonesian Island |journal=Diversity |volume=13 |issue=10 |page=483 |doi=10.3390/d13100483 |issn=1424-2818 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2021Diver..13..483N |hdl=1885/316160 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
